D8 AnthropologyL3 ApplyReference

Egyptian Blue — First Synthetic Pigment (~3000 BCE)

CaCuSi₄O₁₀ (cuprorivaite) fired from sand, limestone, copper, and natron — synthesized 2,500 years before mauveine and still fluoresces in IR today

The blue is from Cu²⁺ d-d transitions in a square-planar oxygen environment. Egyptians fired it at 850–1000 °C; the recipe survived from Old Kingdom Egypt to Roman wall paintings, then was lost for 1,500 years. Modern researchers re-discovered the IR luminescence in 2009 — the pigment glows at 910 nm under red light.

Toggle IR camera view.
